coredata.dc:title: Size-controlled graphene oxide for highly permeable and fouling-resistant outer-selective hollow fiber thin-film composite membranes for forward osmosis
coredata.dc:creator: Lim, Sungil
Park, Kwang Hyun
Tran, Van Huy
Akther, Nawshad
Phuntsho, Sherub
Choi, Joon Young
Shon, Ho Kyong
coredata.dc:description: Size-controlled graphene oxide (SGO) nanosheets, which are uniform and smaller in size than 2 μm, were successfully incorporated into a polyamide (PA) layer for preparing an outer-selective hollow fiber (OSHF) thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane for forward osmosis (FO) applications by vacuum-assisted interfacial polymerization (VAIP). Here, we specifically demonstrate that the SGO nanosheets in amine aqueous solution were horizontally aligned and stacked on the surface of a membrane substrate by vacuum suction from outside to inside in the VAIP; the SGO nanosheets were then well-incorporated into the thin PA layer with less physical damage. In addition, the SGO nanosheets' effective loading inside the PA layer under the VAIP was much higher than that under the typical interfacial polymerization (IP), since there is no issue about the particle loss from air or nitrogen blowing to remove excess amine solution. The benefit would be highly cost-effective in terms of the nanomaterial's use in a TFN membrane production. As a result, the optimum OSHF TFN membrane incorporated with SGO at 0.0005 wt% (SGO5) exhibited outstanding FO performance, including higher water flux at 39.0 L m-2 h-1 and lower specific reverse solute flux at 0.16 g L-1, using a 1 M NaCl draw solution. Furthermore, this study demonstrates the effect of graphene oxide (GO)'s lateral size toward the short water pathway, and GO's stable incorporation and hydrophilicity of the PA thin film. In the fouling test using artificial wastewater, SGO-incorporated membranes exhibited enhanced fouling resistance and cleaning efficiency against the foulant-rich solution. This novel TFN membrane is therefore a good candidate to address FO's challenges for wastewater treatment or desalination.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the hypothesized phenomena concerning GO nanosheet accumulation, arrangement, and PA formation under (a) typical IP and (b) the VAIP for TFN GO membranes, and (c) comparison of the expected water transport across the PA layer incorporated with SGO and BGO.
(a) Size fractionation, (b) SEM images, (c) area distribution, and (d) XPS spectra of BGO and SGO nanosheets.
SEM images of MPD-soaked membrane substrates (SGO0 and SGO5) after the vacuum process for the VAIP.
(a) Morphological images of the PA/SGO composite layer with different GO loadings (0–0.001 wt%) and (b) formation mechanisms of the PA/SGO composite layer, according to SGO loading.

FO performance (water flux and specific reverse solute flux) of OSHF TFN membranes incorporated with GO in the loading range from 0 to 10 g/m3 under the FO operation with a 1 M NaCl solution as DS and DI water as FS.

(a) Profile of water flux and (b) specific reverse solute flux—SRSF of the OSHF TFN membranes, according to different GO loading under different NaCl concentrations from 0.5 to 2 M as DS and DI water as FS.
Comparison of the water flux and SRSF of the OSHF TFC (SGO0) and TFN GO membranes (BGO5 and SGO5) using BGO and SGO nanosheets.
(a) Comparison of the normalized fluxes (left) of SGO0, SGO5, and SGO10 under the FO fouling experiment and (b) (right) membrane fibers after Stage 1 and after the hydraulic cleaning.

Intrinsic transport properties of selected membrane samples according to various GO loading conditions estimated by the FO model algorithm using the profile of water flux and SRSF of the samples as a function of DS concentration.

head.ce:title: Size-controlled graphene oxide for highly permeable and fouling-resistant outer-selective hollow fiber thin-film composite membranes for forward osmosis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Size-controlled graphene oxide (SGO) nanosheets, which are uniform and smaller in size than 2 μm, were successfully incorporated into a polyamide (PA) layer for preparing an outer-selective hollow fiber (OSHF) thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane for forward osmosis (FO) applications by vacuum-assisted interfacial polymerization (VAIP). Here, we specifically demonstrate that the SGO nanosheets in amine aqueous solution were horizontally aligned and stacked on the surface of a membrane substrate by vacuum suction from outside to inside in the VAIP; the SGO nanosheets were then well-incorporated into the thin PA layer with less physical damage. In addition, the SGO nanosheets' effective loading inside the PA layer under the VAIP was much higher than that under the typical interfacial polymerization (IP), since there is no issue about the particle loss from air or nitrogen blowing to remove excess amine solution. The benefit would be highly cost-effective in terms of the nanomaterial's use in a TFN membrane production. As a result, the optimum OSHF TFN membrane incorporated with SGO at 0.0005 wt% (SGO5) exhibited outstanding FO performance, including higher water flux at 39.0 L m-2 h-1 and lower specific reverse solute flux at 0.16 g L-1, using a 1 M NaCl draw solution. Furthermore, this study demonstrates the effect of graphene oxide (GO)'s lateral size toward the short water pathway, and GO's stable incorporation and hydrophilicity of the PA thin film. In the fouling test using artificial wastewater, SGO-incorporated membranes exhibited enhanced fouling resistance and cleaning efficiency against the foulant-rich solution. This novel TFN membrane is therefore a good candidate to address FO's challenges for wastewater treatment or desalination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Forward osmosis (FO) is an osmotically driven membrane process, which is an emerging technology for water and wastewater treatment. Compared to the pressure-driven reverse osmosis (RO) process, FO is considered a low-energy consumption technology because the driving force in its operation is generated by the salinity gradient across a semi-permeable membrane between high-salinity water and low-salinity water. In addition, the FO process is considered to have a lower fouling propensity, due to the absence of hydraulic pressure. Thus, FO technology has been applied into various water purification processes, including desalination of groundwater treatment [1], membrane bioreactor (MBR) for wastewater treatment [2,3], fertilizer dilution for fertigation [4], treatment of the waste backflow liquid in shale-gas exploration [5], removal of trace contaminants in aqueous condition [6], and various hybrid configurations [7,8].

Besides its advantages, some major challenges hinder the commercialization of this FO technology. The absence of an ideal FO membrane is one of the major challenges that limit the application of the FO process for water treatment. An ideal FO membrane should possess: (1) a highly selective active layer for enhancing water permeability; (2) an extremely porous membrane substrate for alleviating internal concentration polarization (ICP); and (3) an anti-fouling property to resist high-fouling feed solution (FS). Many researchers have investigated several strategies to fabricate high-performing thin-film composite FO membranes [9–16]. Apart from developing new FO membranes, many studies have been conducted on membrane modification techniques, so as to either enhance water permeability or mitigate the fouling potential [17,18]. Previous studies have examined the effect of modifying the intrinsic properties of membranes by incorporating various nanomaterials embedded in either the selective layer or membrane substrate [9,19–26]. These nanomaterials include carbon-based nanomaterials (graphene, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots), and inorganic nanoparticles (colloidal silica, silver nanoparticles, titanium oxides) as nanofillers [9,19–26]. In particular, graphene oxide (GO)—a two-dimensional (2D) atomic sheet—has recently been a subject of interest in membrane development [21,22,27–31]. GO has outstanding physico-chemical properties, such as a high aspect ratio with a specific atomic thickness (1–3 nm), and plenty of oxygenated functional groups, including epoxide, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups, which possess hydrophilic and anti-biological natures [27,32]. To date, GO has been applied extensively in the development of nanocomposite membranes for various applications such as water treatment and desalination, batteries, and energy storage solutions [28]. First, GO was itself directly stacked on porous membrane supports using a vacuum filtration, to prepare GO laminates [28,33–35]. Additionally, GO was incorporated into polymer-based membrane substrates to enhance the hydrophilicity, porosity, smoothness, and anti-fouling of the membranes [21,22,27,36]. For thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes, GO was embedded into the thin polyamide (PA) active layer to improve membrane performance. GO incorporation into a dense PA layer increased the internal free volume of the PA layer due to GO's incompatibility with the polymer structure, and the improved surface smoothness and hydrophilicity of the PA layer created low surface tension to water molecules, enhancing water transport [21–23,34,37]. Furthermore, it is expected that the anti-microbial property of GO, as aforementioned, will be able to enhance the resistance of biological growth on the PA-GO composite layer [31].

Recently, we successfully developed the outer-selective hollow fiber (OSHF) thin-film composite (TFC) membrane. It has an outstanding performance for FO application, and has many advantages, as outlined in our previous study [38,39]. During the production of the OSHF TFC FO membranes, a vacuum-assisted interfacial polymerization (VAIP) was specially designed to fabricate a defect-free PA selective layer on the outer surface of membrane substrates. In the VAIP process, specific vacuum pressure was applied to the lumen side of the membrane substrate, in order to remove excess 1,2-phenylenediamine (MPD) solution on the substrates' outer surface. Although this newly developed membrane has proved its high performance in terms of water permeability, rejection, and fouling resistance, as described in our previous study, its performance is yet to be improved by the incorporation of GO nanosheets into the PA selective layer. Given its advantages, mentioned above, the incorporation of GO nanosheets is hypothesized to improve the performance of the current OSHF TFC FO membrane. Under a vacuum condition, GO nanosheets are horizontally aligned and stacked on the outer surface of the membrane substrate. MPD solution can then properly penetrate and occupy space within the GO-stacked layers [28]. At this point, there is less concern about GO's loss from air or nitrogen blowing to remove excess MPD solution on the surface, which means that most deposited GO nanosheets on the membrane surface can be secured by subsequent vacuum supplement. After that, the occupying MPD solution reacts with the TMC solution to fully incorporate GO nanosheets into the PA layer with fewer undesirable voids or cracks. It is, therefore, believed that this modification method will enhance the performance of the existing OSHF TFC FO membrane.

In this study, we specially synthesized size-controlled GO (SGO) nanosheets, which are small and uniformly controlled, to produce stacked GO layers inside a PA thin film, aiming at a short length of water transport of the PA layer, and ideal alignment and high surface area of SGO nanosheets inside the PA layer through the characteristics of SGO nanosheets. Moreover, SGO nanosheets can be more negatively charged than bulk GO (BGO) nanosheets as pristine one, due to abundant oxygenate groups on the edge and surface, which is a result of their high surface area. They can then be more effectively used to alter the surface characteristics of a PA selective layer, being more hydrophilic for low surface energy and high fouling potential [40]. First, the conceptual methodologies and expected mechanisms of the OSHF TFN GO membranes were planned. Second, we successfully produced novel TFN membranes using SGO nanosheets under the VAIP process. Finally, intrinsic properties of developed membrane samples were characterized using the advanced characterization techniques and evaluated using the experimental tests on key FO performance, fouling resistance, and cleaning efficiency under the FO operation, in order to prove their outstanding performance. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study of GO alignment inside the PA layer for the preparation of OSHF TFN membranes. In addition, the size effect of GO inside the PA layer was investigated for water transfer and membrane properties. The results obtained from this study can be considered a guideline for the preparation of OSHF TFN membranes, and a significant contribution to the advancement of TFN membrane fabrication. The newly developed TFN GO membranes prove to be a promising candidate to break the existing trade-off phenomenon between water permeability and solute selectivity, and to enhance fouling resistance and cleaning efficiency in FO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methodologies and hypothesis
Materials and chemicals
ce:section.ce:para: Polyethersulfone (PES, Mw = 62,000–64,000 g/mol, Veradel® 3000P, Solvay) was used to prepare a hollow fiber membrane substrate. The PES polymer was dissolved in 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, Merck). Polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG 400, Mw = 400 g/mol, Sigma-Aldrich) was added to the dope solution as a pore former. A polyamide (PA) selective layer was formed on the substrate by VAIP with reacting MPD with the purity at 99% and trimesoyl chloride (TMC, 98%), manufactured by Sigma-Aldrich. To synthesize SGO and bulk GO (BGO) nanosheets, highly purified graphite (SP-1, Bay carbon) was first used. Subsequently, sulfuric acid (H2SO4, Daejung Chemicals), dihydrogen dioxide (H2O2, Sigma-Aldrich), and potassium permanganate (KMnO4, Sigma-Aldrich) were used to exfoliate the graphite to produce GO. After that, to sort as-prepared GO according to size, the pH in the GO aqueous dispersion was manipulated using hydrogen chloride (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (1 M NaOH) solutions. For the VAIP technique, a vacuum pump (PC 3001 VARIOpro, Vacuubrand) was used to remove the excess MPD solution. TMC was dissolved using n-hexane (over 98% purity, Merck, USA). Membrane substrates were immersed in a glycerol-based aqueous solution (50/50 wt%), to retain their morphology. For FO performance tests, sodium chloride (NaCl, Chem Supply) and deionized water (DI water, Milli-Q, Millipore) were used to prepare draw solution (DS) and feed solution (FS), respectively. For the FO fouling test, the organic-spiked solution was used as a model foulant in the FS. The spiked solution consists of NaCl, calcium chloride (CaCl2, Sigma-Aldrich), sodium alginate (Sigma-Aldrich), and DI water as artificial wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of hollow fiber membrane substrates
ce:section.ce:para: A typical dry-jet wet spinning method was used to fabricate hollow fiber (HF) membrane substrates; this is described in detail elsewhere [41–43]. Following this method, the optimum spinning parameters addressed in our previous study were utilized in preparing a suitable HF membrane substrate for subsequently fabricating OSHF TFN membranes [39]. The suggested spinning parameters are summarized in Table S1.

During the modulation stage, membrane substrate fibers were positioned in the module before coating the PA layer on the outside fibers using the VAIP process. The effective surface area of the membranes in a module is around 6.5 cm2. After conducting the VAIP process for the PA layer formation, the membrane modules were then stored in DI water at 4 °C for characterization and performance testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of OSHF TFN membranes with SGO
ce:section.ce:para: GO nanosheets were produced by exfoliating highly purified graphite through a modified Hummers' method. First, the highly purified commercial graphite (SP-1, Bay carbon) was immersed in sulfuric acid at 98% for 60 min, and the KMnO4 (Sigma-Aldrich) was added in the reactor placed in the ice bath; then, the reactor was moved into the water bath at 30 °C for 120 min. Subsequently, H2O2 at 30% was added into the reactor. The produced GO was filtered using the 0.45 μm microfiltration and washed using a HCl solution. The as-prepared GO nanosheets dispersed in distilled (DI) water were purified using the dialysis membrane against the residual elements. The pH-dependent sorting, which is caused by colloidal stability of the GO nanosheets in the aqueous solution, was selected to obtain SGO nanosheets with thickness uniformity [44]. The sorting from the as-prepared GO samples was conducted by adjusting the pH, using hydrogen chloride (1 M HCl) or sodium hydroxide (1 M NaOH) solutions in the as-prepared GO solution (0.5 mg/mL). GO's size fractionation was optimized to control the GO's lateral size. At pH 2.5, the GO nanosheets were clearly separated into two distinguished stages, as presented in Fig. 2(a). The GO suspensions in each stage were taken by the micropipette. Finally, the obtained GO suspensions were dried using the freeze-drying process after membrane dialysis. The SGO nanosheets extracted from the top stage were denoted as SGO, and the as-prepared GO nanosheets settled down by the pH control were labelled as BGO.

The VAIP process was used to prepare defect-free OSHF TFC membranes in this study, and the detailed protocol of VAIP is described in our literature [39]. Prepared SGO and BGO powders were dispersed in DI water at various GO loadings (0, 0.0001, 0.0003, 0.0005, and 0.0010 wt%) using ultrasonication for over 6 h. MPD at 2 wt% and SDS at 0.2 wt% were simultaneously added and mixed into the GO dispersion. The GO nanosheets were assumed to be horizontally aligned on the membrane surface in the VAIP process under the vacuum condition of 900 mbar. The prepared membrane modules were placed in DI water under low temperature, at 4 °C, before testing the membranes. The OSHF TFN membranes with SGO and BGO nanosheets were denoted as BGO# and SGO#, respectively. The # denotes the last two digits of the GO's concentration, such as SGO0, SGO1, SGO3, SGO5, and up to SGO10.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of membrane substrates
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and chemical properties of SGO and BGO nanosheets were observed using the field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Zeiss Supra 55VP, Carl Zeiss AG) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo Fisher Scientific) analysis, respectively. To analyze the surface morphology and cross-sectional structure of the GO-embedded PA selective layer, the FE-SEM was used under electron energy at 5 or 10 kV. For the FE-SEM analysis, all membrane samples were pretreated with Au/Pd sputtering (EM ACE600, Leica). In addition, the surface topography and roughness of the GO-deposited membrane surface and PA selective layers were observed using atomic force microscopy (AFM, Dimension 3100, Veeco). The surfaces of the samples were scanned at 5 μm under atmosphere tapping. The measurement was duplicated at least three times. To investigate chemical functional groups on the PA layer, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Affinity-1, Shimadzu) was used with a signal in the range of 800 to 4000 cm-1 (signal resolution at 1 cm-1 and minimum 16 scans). The contact angles of the PA active layers were determined using the optical subsystem (Theta Lite 100, Biolin Scientific) with image-analysis software to evaluate the GO effects to enhance the hydrophilicity of the PA layers. At least five measuring points were randomly selected for contact angle analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FO performance of OSHF TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted FO performance tests using the laboratory-scale FO test unit, as described elsewhere [22]. The FO membranes were tested in an active layer facing draw solution (AL-FS) orientation with the draw solution (DS) of 1 M NaCl and the feed solution (FS) of DI water. The detailed experimental methods of the performance tests were addressed in the SI. Water flux and reverse solute flux (RSF) of the membrane samples were determined from the performance tests. Subsequently, specific reverse solute flux (SRSF) was calculated to evaluate membrane performance in the FO processes. The water permeability (A), solute permeability coefficient (B), and intrinsic selectivity (B/A) of hollow fiber TFC membranes were examined using the four-stage prediction model produced by Tiraferri et al. [46], with FO performance results in various DS concentrations, from 0.5 to 2 M. Detailed information of the model prediction is provided elsewhere [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Membrane fouling of hollow fiber TFN GO membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the fouling potential of the membrane samples, simulated artificial wastewater—prepared by blending sodium alginate at 0.2 g L-1, humic acid at 0.2 g L-1, calcium chloride at 0.22 g L-1, and sodium chloride at 1.2 g L-1 in DI water—was used in the fouling test [47]. The filler-free OSHF TFC membrane (SGO0) was examined in the fouling tests as a pristine sample. The stabilization was initially carried out for 30 min using DI water as FS prior to the fouling tests, to obtain control results for comparison. After that, the artificial wastewater was supplied to the feed stream, and a 1 M NaCl solution was continuously circulated in the draw stream. In this condition, the FO unit was operated for 5 h in order to determine the fouling potential as a first cycle. Then, hydraulic washing was immediately conducted in the feed side for 1 h using DI water to evaluate the flux decline of membranes by the foulants in an initial stage and cleaning efficiency after physical cleaning. The feed flow rate in the hydraulic washing at 1.2 L min-1 (40 cm s-1) was three times faster than that in the FO operation under the fouling test at 0.4 L min-1 (13.3 cm s-1). Subsequently, the second-stage fouling tests were conducted for over 8 h using a fresh FS with model foulants in order to examine the fouling potential under long-term operation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hypothesis of OSHF TFN membranes incorporated with SGO
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned previously, IP is a critical process in the preparation of FO membranes in TFC or TFN membranes. In particular, a chemical reaction occurs between the amine-based MPD aqueous solution and the chlorine-based TMC organic solution, resulting in the deposition of a PA active layer on the membrane substrate. To prepare TFN GO membranes, GO nanosheets are well dispersed in a mixture of MPD solution. This GO-mixed MPD solution is then introduced onto the outer surface of the membrane substrate, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). Subsequently, the excess MPD solution on the surface needs to be removed. Many previous studies have employed a conventional technique in which excess MPD solution is normally removed using a nitrogen knife or rubber roller. Following this technique, it is very likely that most GO nanosheets in an excess MPD solution are removed from the surface because of the weak interaction between the GO nanosheets and the substrate's surface produced by the MPD solution's absorption. Therefore, the amount of effective GO loading required in the PA layer could be overestimated due to the massive loss of GO. Most literature in this area reports that the optimum GO loading for TFN GO membranes is generally placed in the range of 0.005–0.02 wt% [11,48–50], which is somewhat higher than this study considered. Another issue is that the remaining GO nanosheets could be randomly positioned on the surface prior to reacting with the TMC solution. When the PA selective layer is formed, randomly aligned GO nanosheets might give rise to the deterioration of the integrity of the PA layer. This negatively affects the membrane's selectivity as well as the osmotic driving force. Therefore, GO positioning inside the PA layer should be carefully controlled for the preparation of high-performance TFN GO membranes.

Unlike a typical IP process, the VAIP process could facilitate the accumulation and arrangement of GO nanosheets on the membrane substrate's surface. Our hypothesis is that vacuum pressure effectively attaches and horizontally arranges GO nanosheets on the membrane surface. Moreover, due to the absence of air blowing, the GO nanosheets lost during this VAIP process are substantially fewer compared to the typical IP technique. However, this does not mean that GO nanosheets are homogeneously distributed or fully covered on the membrane surface. As shown in Fig. 1 (b), the MPD-GO aqueous mixture is introduced onto the membrane surface for a certain time. A certain value of vacuum pressure is then applied to the lumen side of the fiber to remove excess MPD solution. At the same time, due to its 2D structure, GO nanosheets can be easily accumulated, stacked, and strongly attached to the membrane surface under the vacuum condition. This implies that most GO nanosheets could effectively be incorporated inside the PA layer during the VAIP process. The optimum GO loading in the VAIP will be thus much lower than would be the case in the typical IP. Therefore, using our concept, GO fillers can be efficiently and economically used to develop TFN membranes by saving the capital cost of GO. Furthermore, with the optimum loading of GO nanosheets, the MPD aqueous solution would be properly occupied in both membrane pores and the interlayer spaces of stacked GO sheets due to GO's strong water affinity [28,32]. The stacked GO layers are thus likely to be swelled by occupied MPD aqueous solution inside the spaces between GO's floors unlike graphene, and the PA material will be subsequently occupied well inside the GO's interlayer spaces by a proper reaction with the TMC solution [28]. To further improve the performance of TFN GO membranes, our hypothesis is that small- and uniform-sized SGO nanosheets are preferable to BGO nanosheets, as the SGO nanosheets may ideally create the stacked GO layers inside a PA thin film with a shorter water pathway, as well as less heterogeneous placement of the GO layers to enhance water permeability while retaining ion selectivity, as shown in Fig. 1(c). Moreover, SGO nanosheets can be more hydrophilic than BGO due to abundant oxygenate groups on their edges and surfaces, which can be more effectively used to alter the surface characteristics of a PA selective layer, given that they are more hydrophilic for low surface energy and high fouling potential [40]. Therefore, we firmly believe that our hypothesis can strongly contribute to finding the best strategy to develop TFN GO membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Characteristics of SGO nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, SGO and BGO nanosheets were used for the preparation of OSHF TFN GO membranes. Fig. 2 presents the intrinsic characteristics of the SGO and BGO nanosheets based on FESEM and XPS analysis. In particular, Fig. 2 (a) presents the graphical schematic of size fractionation of GO nanosheets by pH control, and Fig. 2 (b) and (c), respectively, show the morphological shapes and area distributions of BGO and SGO nanosheets with Gauss fittings. Under the acid condition at pH 2.5, the GO nanosheets were fractionated and sedimented according to the GO's lateral area. Using the SEM images, the area distributions of BGO and SGO were estimated using ImageJ software. As a result, the area distribution of BGO varied widely, from 0 to 14 μm2, compared to that of SGO; however, the area range of SGO was much less than that of BGO, which is clearly distinguished in the figure. SGO was uniformly controlled, with the mean lateral area at around 0.65 μm2, and its area distribution range was under 2.5 μm2. According to the XPS results (Fig. 2 (d)), SGO exhibited a higher oxygen peak (O1s) at 33.0% than that of BGO at 27.9% due to SGO's high surface area, which is correlated with the intensity of oxygen functional groups. In contrast, a portion of the carbon peak (C1s) of the SGO nanosheets was relatively reduced compared to that of BGO. By deconvoluting the C1s region, detailed functionalities of BGO and SGO nanosheets were investigated in Fig. 4(d). By comparing the peaks of BGO and SGO nanosheets, the ratio of the epoxy groups (C-O-C) declined from 48.9% (BGO) to 35.4% (SGO) after reducing the lateral size, as the epoxy groups (mostly placed on the lateral surface of GO nanosheets) were broken by the acid treatment. However, the ratio of the carbonyl (C=O) and carboxylate groups (O-C=O) increased due to an increase in the surface area of the SGO nanosheets edges. Therefore, the oxygen functionalities of the SGO nanosheets were more increased than those of the BGO nanosheets. On the other hand, the peak of non-oxygenated carbon rings (C-C) in the SGO nanosheets was retained compared to that of pristine BGO, which means that SGO nanosheets retained their original GO properties even though they were chemically treated. Regarding the chemical composition of SGO and BGO nanosheets, this is preferable to alleviate foulant attraction on the membrane surface via high surface charge when SGO nanosheets are incorporated into a PA active layer. Moreover, as mentioned previously, smaller SGO nanosheets may decrease the travel distance of water transport inside a dense PA layer due to their small lateral sizes, as presented in Fig. 1(c). Furthermore, smaller SGO nanosheets may also retain the integrity of the PA composite layer against physical damage. Therefore, SGO nanosheets could be positively utilized as nanofillers to enhance the membrane's performance and fouling mitigation through their physico-chemical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of OSHF TFN GO membranes
Alignment of GO nanosheets on a membrane substrate
ce:section.ce:para:
For the VAIP process, excess MPD solution on the substrate's surface is removed from outside to inside under vacuum pressure. As per our hypothesis for GO alignment in Fig. 1, GO nanosheets are forcibly attached to the membrane substrate during the VAIP process. To confirm this hypothesis of GO accumulation and arrangement, a sample of MPD/GO-soaked membrane substrate was prepared and analyzed using FE-SEM. Fig. 3
presents the SEM images of GO-free membrane substrate (SGO0) and GO-stacked membrane substrate (SGO5) after the vacuum process in the VAIP. In the GO-free sample, no particle or external particulate was found on the surface of the membrane substrate. However, some GO-stacked plates were found on the surface of the membrane substrate stacked with GO nanosheets. The GO-stacked plates were partially covered on the membrane substrate. In particular, GO nanosheets were overlapped (SGO5_1) together or scattered as a single sheet (SGO5_2) on the membrane surface after the vacuum process, as presented in Fig. 3. Although GO-stacked plates were not homogenously placed on the surface, it is clearly confirmed that the GO nanosheets were horizontally aligned and accumulated on the surface after the vacuum process. By virtue of their proper arrangement inside the PA layer, the PA active layer incorporated with GO nanosheets is less prone to defects when it is formed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of OSHF TFN GO membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 (a) presents SEM images of the PA selective layers of all OSHF TFC and TFN membrane samples. As shown in the images, SGO0 membranes showed the typical morphology of their PA layer like a ridge-and-valley structure, as expected. SGO1, 3, and 5 membranes demonstrated PA structures similar to that of the SGO0 membrane. In addition, SGO was not visible on the PA surface, suggesting that the SGO nanosheets were well embedded into the thin PA layer. The SEM results show that the loading of GO nanosheets less than 0.0005 wt% presented surface morphologies of the PA layer similar to the preparation of OSHF TFN membranes. However, the PA layer became slightly smoother, with the formation of leaf-like structures, due to the chemical resistance from SGO nanosheets in the IP reaction when the SGO loading was increased. In addition, it was interesting to note that the black-colored plates were observed on the SGO10 membrane surface only, as presented in Fig. 4 (a), and the lateral sizes of the plates were observed in the range of 2–10 μm. Based on the SEM analysis, the formation mechanism of the PA/SGO composite layer, according to SGO loading, was hypothesized and is presented in Fig. 4 (b). For the SGO samples of less than 0.0005 wt% (SGO1, 3, and 5), SGO nanosheets were incorporated into the PA layer under the VAIP process. In addition, as the GO loading increased to 0.0005 wt%, the average roughness seemed to be slightly decreased due to the hindrance of the IP reaction by the GO-stacked layers. However, some of the SGO nanosheets might have been aggregated and overloaded during the vacuum process, when SGO loading was 0.001 wt% for SGO10. Subsequently, the chemical reaction with MPD and TMC might have been affected by the overloaded thick GO layers accumulated on the membrane surface, so that the PA structures on the plates were relatively sunken and much smoother than that of a pristine PA layer, as presented in Fig. 3(b); as a consequence, the membrane's performance deteriorated. Therefore, the optimum SGO loading should not exceed 0.0005 wt%, in order to sustain the typical morphology of the PA layer.

Fig. S2 presents the ATR-FTIR spectra and contact angle of OSHF TFC and TFN membranes incorporated with SGO nanosheets into the PA selective layer. In Fig. S2 (a) and (b), the spectra of all membrane samples exhibited some peaks of the amine group at around 1650 (C=O stretching) and 1530 cm-1 (N-H bending), in addition to aryl ether stretching at around 1220 cm-1, which represents a typical infrared spectroscopy (IR) spectrum of a PA thin-film composite [51]. Compared to the SGO0 TFC membranes, the spectra of the TFN GO membranes showed several different peaks in the spectrum. In particular, for the GO-embedded TFN membranes, specific peaks at 820–860 cm-1 for epoxy compounds, at 1020 and 1070 cm-1 for hydroxyl compounds, and at around 2,820, 2,910, and 3500 cm-1 for carboxyl compounds were found. These are representative bonding compounds of GO nanosheets, which means that GO nanosheets were well incorporated inside the PA selective layer. Notably, the peak intensity of the hydroxyl and epoxy compounds was proportionally increased when GO loading was increased. As shown in Fig. S2 (c), for a membrane's hydrophilicity, the contact angle of TFN GO membranes was slightly decreased, from around 80°–78°, with an increase of SGO from 0 to 0.0003 wt%. However, the contact angle of SGO5 dropped drastically to 69.5°, and SGO10 resulted in the lowest contact angle at 63.3°. Although this CA decline was attributed to the hydrophilic nature of the GO nanosheets, it should be noted that SGO nanosheets of less than 0.0005 wt% cannot effectively enhance the hydrophilicity of the OSHF TFN membranes, since a small number of SGO nanosheets do not affect whole membrane areas. It can therefore be concluded that SGO loading beyond 0.0005 wt% can effectively improve membrane's hydrophilicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of OSHF TFN GO membranes
FO performances
ce:section.ce:para: The FO performance of the membrane samples was evaluated for water flux and SRSF under an active layer facing feed solution (AL-FS) (called the FO mode) using a 1 M NaCl as DS and DI water as FS in the lab-scale FO test unit, as shown in Fig. 5 . The SGO0 membrane exhibited relatively high water flux at 25.6 L m-2 h-1 and low SRSF at 0.21 g L-1, indicating that the PA layer produced by our VAIP technique was well deposited on the outer surface of the HF membrane substrates [39]. For TFN GO membranes, the water fluxes of all SGO membranes with the SGO loading up to 0.0005 wt% were higher than that of the SGO0, without sacrificing their ion selectivity. When the SGO loading in the MPD solution increased, the water flux of the prepared membranes slightly and proportionally increased with the slight decrease in their SRSF. Interestingly, SGO5 showed the highest water flux at 39.0 L m-2 h-1, which was more than 55% of SGO0 at 25.1 L m-2 h-1 with a decrease in SRSF from 0.21 to 0.16 g L-1. SGO5 exhibited the most outstanding FO performance with the highest water flux and lowest SRSF under FO operation, as SGO nanosheets at the optimum loading contributed to enhancing the water transport by increasing the free volume and hydrophilicity of the PA layer, and to shortening the water pathway inside the PA layer by the small lateral size of SGO, which will be further investigated in the next chapter. Moreover, the ion selectivity of SGO5 was well retained, with no additional damage by SGO; this was proved by the hypothesis and experimental results. On the other hand, by increasing the SGO loading beyond 0.0005 wt%, SGO10 exhibited reduced water flux to 29.0 L m-2 h-1, and its SRSF peaked at 0.31 g L-1. The overloaded SGO nanosheets of SGO10 caused the FO performance to deteriorate, due to the physical defects from aggregated SGO nanosheets, as depicted in our hypothesis and characterizations (Fig. 4). The results imply that the successful incorporation of SGO into the PA layer could overcome the trade-off relationship between water permeability and reverse solute diffusion in FO processes.

Fig. 6 presents comparative profiles of the water flux and SRSF of the OSHF TFC and TFN membranes under the FO operation as a function of DS concentrations from 0.5 to 2 M NaCl. As the osmotic pressure increased, the water fluxes of all membranes gradually increased because of the increase in the driving force. On the other hand, the SRSF trends of the membrane samples were stable according to the variation of DS concentration. This means that the ion selectivity of all membranes was stably maintained regardless of the difference of osmotic pressure. SGO5, as the optimal membrane, exhibited the highest water flux at 50.2 L m-2 h-1 with its low SRSF of 0.16 g L-1 with 2 M NaCl as DS and DI water as FS. The FO model algorithm was applied in order to predict the intrinsic transport properties of the OSHF GO membranes using the FO performance results (Fig. 6) instead of determining the properties using an RO mode operation, due to concerns about membrane damage and incorrect results from the RO mode operation because of its different transport mechanism and hydraulic conditions compared to those in the FO mode operation [46,52]. Table 1 shows estimated values of the water permeability (A), solute permeability coefficient (B), and intrinsic selectivity (B/A) of selected membrane samples as pristine (SGO0), optimum (SGO5), and overloaded (SGO10). The A value of the SGO5 membrane was 5.71 L-1m-2h-1bar-1, which was much higher than those of other membranes. Although the B value of SGO5 was the highest, at 0.81 L-1m-2h-1bar-1, the intrinsic selectivity was the lowest, at 0.14 bar, due to its outstandingly high A value. In contrast to SGO5, SGO10 exhibited the highest intrinsic selectivity, at 0.3 bar, due to the poor integrity of its PA layer caused by the overloading of SGO nanosheets. Generally, the intrinsic selectivity of an ideal FO membrane should be as low as possible, which means that high A and low B values are desirable for ideal membrane properties. Therefore, it can be clearly stated that SGO5 possesses the most excellent water permeability and solute selectivity under the FO operating condition of all the OSHF membrane samples.

The FO performance of our homemade OSHF FO membranes (SGO0 and SGO5) was compared to other homemade and commercial HF FO membranes from the literature as shown in Table S2 [15,41,53–58]. In the comparison, most homemade inner selective hollow fiber (ISHF) membranes presented good FO performances. In particular, the homemade aquaporin membrane showed the best FO performance in comparison with the highest water flux, at 49.1 L m-2 h-1, and the lowest SRSF values, at 0.10 g L-1, when FS and DS were DI water and 1 M NaCl, respectively. However, the aquaporin HF membranes are ISHF TFC membranes with a small inner diameter; therefore, their application could be limited depending on the feed water's quality. In the comparison, the OSHF TFC and CTA membranes in the literature could not present good FO performance, which means that formation of an active layer on the outer surface of a HF substrate is still challenging. However, the pristine OSHF TFC (SGO0) membranes exhibited relatively good FO performance. Subsequently, with the incorporation of SGO nanosheets, the optimum SGO5 membrane exhibited high water flux (39.0 L-1 m-2 h-1) and good ion selectivity of SGO5 (0.16 g L-1) in this comparison. For commercial HF membranes, the recently launched aquaporin HFFO2 membrane exhibited better FO performance than that of existing commercial cellulose triacetate (CTA) HF membranes manufactured by Toyobo, Japan, with extremely low water flux, at 8.0 L-1 m-2 h-1, due to its dense internal structure. However, the FO performances of these commercial membranes need to be further improved in order to demonstrate commercial FO applications. Therefore, we believe that our approach to membrane fabrication can contribute to the advancement of FO applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size effect of GO nanosheets for FO performance
ce:section.ce:para: In the results above, OSHF TFN membranes with SGO exhibited excellent FO performance under the optimum conditions, as small and uniform SGO nanosheets inside the PA layer might positively enhance water transport with no sacrifice of their ion selectivity compared to BGO nanosheets. To investigate the effect of GO nanosheet size on FO performance, both BGO and SGO nanosheets were used in the preparation of OSHF TFN membranes. The optimum SGO loading was confirmed at 0.0005 wt%, so the BGO loading was also fixed at this value for the comparison. Thus, the prepared samples using BGO nanosheets were denoted as BGO5. Fig. 7 presents a comparison of water fluxes and SRSFs of SGO0, BGO5, and SGO5 under the FO operation, using 1 M NaCl and DI water. As a result, the GO-embedded membranes (BGO5 and SGO5) exhibited higher water flux, at 29.1 L m-2 h-1, with retained SRSF at around 0.23 g L-1, than those of SGO0, due to the GO's contribution to enhancing water transport through improved hydrophilicity and the increased free volume of the PA/GO composite layer from GO's natural characteristics, as mentioned previously. In the comparison between BGO5 and SGO5, SGO5 showed the best FO performance with the highest water flux of 38.9 L m-2 h-1, and a similar reverse solute flux at 6.36 g m-2 h-1 with SGO0 (5.33 g m-2 h-1) and BGO5 (6.57 g m-2 h-1). The trend of the FO performance was in good agreement with our hypothesis in this study, indicating that SGO may produce less transport resistance and a shorter pathway for water transport than that of BGO, due to the difference in their lateral size, as presented in Fig. 1 (c). Furthermore, the enhanced water permeability of SGO5 might be attributed to the higher free volume of its dense PA layer, since SGO nanosheets possess a higher surface area than BGO nanosheets at the same mass concentration. Therefore, we conclude that small- and uniform-sized SGO nanosheets in the PA layer are likely to contribute to the improvement of the PA skin layer's water permeability while maintaining its intensity. This means that the trade-off relationship between water permeability and selectivity could be ruled out by this approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fouling and cleaning study of OSHF TFN GO membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effect of SGO nanosheets on the fouling resistance of the OSHF TFN GO membrane under FO operation, fouling resistance and cleaning efficiency tests were conducted on selected membrane samples (SGO0, SGO5, and SGO10), using the artificial wastewater as FS and 1 M NaCl as DS. Fig. 8 (a) presents the trends of the flux decline and cleaning efficiency of the selected membrane samples and pictures of the membrane fibers after Stage 1 and hydraulic cleaning. In the short-term fouling test (Stage 1), SGO10 showed the lowest flux decline, at around 10% in all samples, due to the electric repulsion between negatively charged ions on the surface of the SGO nanosheets and model foulants; however, the flux decline of SGO5 was higher than that of SGO10 and slightly lower than that of SGO0. These results imply that SGO loading at 0.001 wt% was quite efficient for fouling mitigation. After Stage 1, for membrane cleaning, the hydraulic flushing under the cross-flow condition at 40 cm s-1 was immediately conducted for all membrane samples for 1 h. GO-embedded samples (SGO5 and SGO10) exhibited excellent flux recovery, at around 98% after the hydraulic flushing, but the GO-free TFC sample (SGO0) presented relatively low flux recovery, at 86%. The lower fouling and higher recovery of SGO5 and SGO10 were attributed to the weak and loose interaction between negatively charged foulants and their modified surface by the SGO nanosheets, which means that irreversible fouling of these GO samples rarely occurred. As shown in Fig. 8 (b), the foulants in SGO5 and SGO10 were almost washed out after the cleaning due to the loose interaction between the membrane surface and the foulants. However, some of the brown-colored foulants in SGO0 remained on the fiber surface. In Stage 2, the flux decline of all membranes exhibited similar trends to those in Stage 1. After 6 h in Stage 2, the normalized flux of the SGO0 sample was significantly decreased, at 0.69 more than that of SGO5 and SGO10, which were both around 0.82, due to its higher fouling potential and irreversible fouling. As presented in Fig. 8 (b), the photos of the membrane fibers before and after the hydraulic cleaning support the statements in terms of the fouling results. As mentioned previously, one of the specific advantages of the OSHF membrane is the ease of membrane cleaning on its outer surface by high-flow hydraulic flushing. Although both SGO5 and SGO10 presented significant fouling mitigation and cleaning efficiency, as shown in Fig. 8, the FO performance of SGO10 was worse than those of SGO0 and SGO5, due to its poor selectivity, which may cause accelerating membrane fouling [59]. This suggests, therefore, that SGO5 is the best candidate to alleviate membrane fouling in this study. Furthermore, as anti-biological activities within GO, the existence of SGO nanosheets inside the PA layer may interfere with the growth of microorganisms on the PA layer [27,60]. Overall, the behavior of SGO5 can accelerate the development of a membrane's novelty and specialty for fouling mitigation and effective cleaning, as well as to improve FO performance in future FO applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, a novel concept and mechanism have been hypothesized, followed by a demonstration of novel OSHF TFN membrane development using SGO nanosheets. Initially, GO nanosheet loading incorporated into a PA selective layer of OSHF TFN membrane was optimized using the VAIP process. A comparison of the performance of two SGO nanosheets and BGO nanosheets was subsequently carried out. The findings and conclusions are presented below.
(1)
The newly hypothesized concept and mechanism were successfully demonstrated based on experimental results and analysis. Under the VAIP process, most SGO nanosheets in the MPD solution were properly accumulated and horizontally aligned on the outer surface of the membrane substrate. This facilitated the formation of a defect-free PA selective layer incorporated with SGO nanosheets during the interfacial polymerization process. As expected, GO-embedded TFN membranes exhibited improving permeability without trade-off of ion selectivity, due to the enhanced free volume and hydrophilicity of the PA selective layer.
(2)
Different SGO nanosheets loading conditions were used and optimized; the SGO5 membrane sample exhibited the highest performances (water flux = 39.0 L m-2 h-1; and SRSF = 0.16 g L-1) under a pairing of 1 M NaCl and DI water. This indicated that the optimum SGO loading for the OSHF TFN membrane preparation was extremely low, at 0.0005 wt%, due to a much-reduced loss of SGO in the VAIP. GO-embedded TFN membranes presented strong fouling resistance and high cleaning efficiency using the wastewater due to the loose accumulation of foulants on the surface. In particular, the short-term hydraulic flushing was considered effective for washing attached foulants on the surface, with an outstanding flux recovery at around 98%. In addition, it is expected that the membrane may possess an anti-bacterial property due to the GO nanosheets.
(3)
Membranes that incorporated SGO nanosheets offered better performance compared to those of BGO under similar operating conditions. The attributed reason is that SGO may produce a shorter pathway for water transport due to its small size, contributing to the improvement of the PA skin layer's water permeability while maintaining its selectivity.

These findings prove that the optimum OSHF TFN membrane (SGO5) could be the most suitable candidate among existing outstanding FO membranes not only for membrane performance but also because of its scalability in various wastewater treatments and water reuse applications.
